Even though at the heart of it, VFLO is more of a value play, its portfolio construction also involves eradicating low growth (in terms of sales, EBITDA, and EPS) FCF yielders, which also makes it suitable for those who want stocks with respectable growth profiles.

What Are Some Of The Alternatives To FLOW?

Two close peers of VFLO that also construct portfolios on the basis of the FCF yield are the Global X U.S. Cash Flow Kings 100 ETF (FLOW), and the Pacer US Cash Cows 100 ETF (COWZ).

The latter is the most established and popular product in this space with a listing history of close to nine years, AUM of almost $20B, and the tightest bid-ask spreads of just 0.02% (reflecting the liquidity seen in this ticker. It also covers the widest portfolio of stocks (102) and tilts mainly to large-caps unlike the other two. COWZ also tilts towards the enticing technology sector. However, note that COWZ is the only one of the three that pays dividends on a quarterly basis, and it also comes across as the least cost efficient (an expense ratio of 0.49%). The annual churn of over 150% is also quite concerning.

FLOW appears to be a very low-profile product with less than $25M in AUM and wide bid-ask spreads of 0.19% (it is also the youngest of the three products). However, it deserves some credit for being the most cost efficient, the most lucrative from a yield perspective (like VFLO, it too pays monthly) and also witnesses the lowest degree of churn (although still high by general ETF standards). FLOW also stands out for tilting towards consumer cyclical stocks unlike the other two.

Two areas where VFLO stands out from these alternatives is that it is the only one which also incorporates a forward-looking screening of expected FCF (the other two base the portfolio on trailing FCF), and it also doesn’t take into account REIT stocks.

Summary

VFLO, a narrow portfolio (50 stocks) built around the thesis of expected free cash flow, offers a rare blend of quality, value and growth stocks while also incorporating a useful forward-looking viewpoint and a monthly distribution schedule. While its elaborate construction and sizeable churn may put off investors who appreciate stability, studies have shown that stocks that score highly on the basis of expected FCF, tend to outperform the rest of the pack.

This article answers these three main questions about VFLO:

  • How does VFLO build its portfolio of stocks?
  • Who is VFLO meant for and what are the risks they could face?
  • How does VFLO compare to other funds that use FCF in the selection process?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: The Only Quantum You Can Use Today, But I Would Still Sell
data.description:
Introduction

In my previous breakdown of the quantum computing industry, I argued that the “Holy Grail” general-purpose quantum computer (the one that could crack every encryption and simulate molecules at a scale unimaginable for today’s machines) is still years, maybe decades away. I recommend you read that piece first if you are interested in the industry, because I tried to go deep into how it all works and why we may never see this stronger-than-all computer. In this article, I will talk about annealing and the gate model. The previous piece described the gate model in detail, while this one will focus on annealing.

D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE:QBTS) is the only public pure-play quantum company that is actually generating revenue today from its customers. But it’s not building the type of quantum computers most people imagine when they hear the word “quantum.” Instead, it focuses on a very different approach (quantum annealing), which is something entirely different from what I explained yesterday. It has different technology, commercial potential, and as a result, the stock will behave slightly differently.

Annealing vs. Gate Model

Most of the names I discussed in my previous piece, like IBM (IBM), Google (GOOGL), IonQ (IONQ), and Quantinuum (HON), are focused on building gate-model quantum computers. These are the machines that, once error-corrected (something I called logical qubits in the last article) and scaled to hundreds of thousands of qubits, could revolutionize cryptography, chemistry, and countless other fields. They are basically building that imaginary quantum computer that will be stronger than all of the computers in the world combined and will be hacking into every encryption. But there is a tiny problem. Nobody is even remotely close to that, and there are no guarantees someone ever will be. It’s not that we are years away. If some major technological or scientific breakthrough doesn’t happen soon, we could be decades away.

D-Wave, however, uses quantum annealing, which is entirely different, and this is the reason it can start commercial operations already today. It is quantum-powered, but it’s not what you initially imagine when you hear quantum computer, and it never will be. The whole concept is much simpler: you start with qubits in a kind of “fluid” state, and instead of manipulating them step by step like in the gate model (manipulating them is the biggest challenge), you let the system naturally settle into the lowest-energy configuration.

That lowest-energy state often represents the best solution to an optimization problem. It could solve a problem of routing trucks across a city or scheduling thousands of workers across shifts. In general, it’s problems where there are millions of possible combinations, but one or two that are more efficient.

So D-Wave isn’t trying to solve all of quantum’s promises. Its focus is on optimization, logistics, some machine learning applications, and simulations. Management stopped at the beginning of the latest call to insist that annealing isn’t a niche approach, and on this point, they’re right. But there were a couple of points where their explanation is stretched. In general, I wouldn’t take a word from an interested party for the whole truth, but it relates in general to all public companies and their managers. Let’s see the quote first, and then discuss it:

Before turning to commercial updates, I wanted to take a moment to remind everyone of the differences between annealing and gate model, as there still appears to be continued misinformation that we believe is confusing the market.

Annealing and gate model are different types of quantum computing approaches that solve different types of problems. According to peer-reviewed research, gate model quantum computers will not offer advantages for all problem types. Multiple research results have shown that annealing quantum computers outperform and are expected to continue to outperform gate model on optimization problems. So gate model cannot universally solve all problems better than classical.

It is also important to understand that annealing is not a niche solution. We believe that annealing quantum computing is well suited to a broad set of problems, including AI machine learning, quantum simulation and business optimization, which is ubiquitous in today's modern enterprise. Business optimization problems encompass things like workforce scheduling, production scheduling, resource allocation, vehicle routing and so on.

Together, these represent extensive use cases with far-reaching potential. To characterize annealing quantum computing as niche is misleading and ill-informed. Both annealing and gate model quantum computers can solve a broad range of problems and each has its limitations with problems it cannot efficiently solve.

Annealing quantum computing is very good at solving optimization problems, which cannot be efficiently solved by gate model. Gate model quantum computers, once commercialized, are expected to be very good at quantum chemistry and 3D fluid dynamics, which cannot be efficiently solved by annealing. And both systems, both systems can tackle linear algebra and factorization, meaning problems related to AI, machine learning and cryptography. We believe that organizations will need both annealing and gate model systems in order to address their full problem sets. This is why D-Wave is building both types of quantum computers for our customers.

What the management is right about is that it’s not really “niche.” The optimization is everywhere, and it’s crucial for many huge, global industries. The idea that annealing could become the specialized tool that businesses use makes sense. If gate-model quantum computers eventually arrive at scale, they could replace the annealing, but it’s very early to talk about it. There will be many important details, like scaling, cost-effectiveness, use cases, etc.

Where I think management stretches the story is in how fast this coexistence becomes commercially meaningful. They’re right that optimization problems are everywhere, but the difference between quantum computers being theoretically better at this than regular computers, and someone actually ready to pay hundreds of millions for it, is huge. One of the major concerns in the quantum industry is whether it will be cost-effective.

What is interesting is that management, while protecting annealing, said they are working on both. So it means they recognize the need to work on the gate model. What management did not say is that their efforts on this are far behind competitors like IBM, Google, and Quantinuum. It’s a big and very expensive problem to solve. I described it in the previous piece.

A Working Product in the Market

But short term, there are many advantages to what D-Wave is doing. Unlike IonQ or Rigetti (RGTI), which are still mostly research-driven with cloud demos, D-Wave’s Advantage2 system is already commercially available. Customers can buy access through the cloud or even install on-premise systems, as the Julich Supercomputing Center in Germany has done.

Advantage2 has shown meaningful technical progress over the previous generation, with double coherence times, 40% more energy scale, and higher connectivity between qubits. D-Wave claims to have achieved a kind of “quantum supremacy” over regular computers. Whether D-Wave’s claims of supremacy will stand the test of time is still up for debate, but the direction looks good. They have customers like E.ON, GE Vernova, Nikon, and NTT Data, and even academic and government partners like the U.K.’s National Quantum Computing Center and the University of Oxford. It has also built proofs of concept in aerospace, where a Fortune 500 client is testing a dozen optimization cases, and even in law enforcement, where North Wales Police used D-Wave’s system to optimize patrol car deployment.

Financial Results

In the latest quarter, D-Wave reported $3.1 million in revenue, up 42% year over year. Bookings reached $1.3 million, almost double the prior year’s quarter, and gross margins is pretty high at over 60%.

GAAP gross margin for the second quarter was 63.8%, representing a slight improvement from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 GAAP gross margin of 63.6%. Non-GAAP gross margin for the second quarter was 71.8%, a slight decrease of 1.3% from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 non-GAAP gross margin of 73.1%.

But the losses are much bigger. On a GAAP basis, the company has a net loss of $167 million, though most of that came from warrant revaluation. On an adjusted basis, the loss was closer to $25 million, which still reflects the heavy cost of R&D and scaling. Adjusted EBITDA loss was $20 million for the quarter.

Net loss for the second quarter was $167.3 million or $0.55 per share, an increase of $149.5 million or $0.45 per share from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 net loss of $17.8 million or $0.10 a share. The increase in the net loss was primarily due to $142 million in noncash nonoperating charges related to the remeasurement of the company's warrant liability as well as realized losses stemming from actual warrant exercises.

The one bright spot is the balance sheet, but investors won’t really like that. D-Wave now holds over $800 million in cash, its strongest liquidity position ever. That came at the cost of heavy dilution. Shares outstanding have increased significantly from around 127 million at the end of 2022 to over 340 million today. Investors who bought earlier have already paid for this liquidity.

Valuation is extraordinary to put lightly. With a market cap of nearly $9.5 billion and revenues barely over $15 million for the first half of 2025, D-Wave trades at over 350x forward sales. For context, that’s more expensive than all of the Nvidia’s, Palantirs, and whatever expensive stock you can think of during the AI hype. This stock is not valued like a confident player it could become in optimization, but like a company that created the first quantum computer and took over the world with it. In the past. And now it’s just owns everything.

Jokes aside, I don’t think investors understand what they are actually buying if they drive this stock to a high valuation like this. And I don’t blame them. Quantum computing is this magical unicorn no one understands, but also almost no one is trying to. I have read so many articles and valuations of different quantum stocks and the sector as a whole, but I realized people are not talking about what we really invest in. There is no discussion of whether what they are waiting for is really possible to achieve. It doesn’t, by the way, mean I did not like D-Wave. It’s an interesting company. But with their roadmap as of today, I don’t think they would be fairly valued at this level if they have already achieved everything planned for the next few years.

Future Possibilities

The bullish story around D-Wave really comes down to a few things. First, they have a commercial head start. No one has that really yet. D-Wave is already selling systems and offering cloud access to customers today.

Second, the management has said that they are also working on a gate model. I am not sure if it's that great of idea given how far they are behind, but if they pull that off together with the annealing, they could create a very unusual position in the sector. And third, the U.S. government, Japan, South Korea, and parts of Europe have already opened the door to partnerships. Defense, logistics, and energy optimization are exactly the kind of problems annealing is well-suited to, and if those institutions decide this technology is worth backing, D-Wave could benefit disproportionately.

But there’s a bearish side as well. Annealing may turn out to be too narrow in scope. Or not cost-effective. If gate-model systems begin scaling, they could replace annealing altogether. And even if both approaches do coexist, D-Wave is years behind in gate-model, competing against giants like IBM, Google, and Microsoft with their huge R&D budgets.

The company survives by selling more stock, and that comes at the expense of existing shareholders. Finally, the valuation is already so high as if D-Wave will dominate optimization and stay relevant in gate-model at the same time.

Conclusion

D-Wave is not “that” quantum computer. Not the one that will crack RSA encryption or model new drugs. But it is a quantum company (not a computer) with a product on the market today.

For investors, the trade-off is clear. On the one hand, there is real technology, real customers, and a lot of cash. On the other hand, there is a $9.5 billion market cap against tiny revenues, intense dilution, and the huge risk that annealing turns out to be a technological dead-end.

If you’re bullish, well, I think you are very brave to buy it now. If you’re bearish, you see this speculative bubble priced decades ahead of its time. For me, it’s a fascinating company to follow, a pioneer in proving that quantum can deliver value today, but at these valuations, it’s a lottery ticket rather than an investment.

Note: I never short stocks, and I wouldn’t start with something that’s already up more than 2,000% in a year. My Sell rating simply means that if I had been holding it from earlier, I would be taking profits now.
